content: Controversies around R&D cost estimates include: DiMasi study excluded drugs developed without industry funding (e.g., NIH-discovered), used non-public data from pharmaceutical companies, included substantial opportunity cost assumptions (cost of capital during development), and counted marketing expenditures as R&D in some analyses. Public funding contributes significantly to drug discovery—NIH funds ~$40 billion annually in biomedical research, underlying many approved drugs. Studies show ~50% of new molecular entities have origins in public-sector research. Price-R&D relationship is questioned: pharmaceutical companies spend more on marketing than R&D (approximately 25% vs 15-20% of revenue), profit margins exceed most industries (15-20% net), and price increases often exceed inflation without corresponding R&D investment. Policy proposals include: Medicare price negotiation, international reference pricing, R&D transparency requirements, and delinking R&D costs from product prices.
